coredata.dc:title: Graphene-derived antibacterial nanocomposites for water disinfection: Current and future perspectives
coredata.dc:creator: Omran, Basma
Baek, Kwang-Hyun
coredata.dc:description: Antimicrobial nanomaterials provide numerous opportunities for the synthesis of next-generation sustainable water disinfectants. Using the keywords graphene and water disinfection and graphene antibacterial activity, a detailed search of the Scopus database yielded 198 and 1433 studies on using graphene for water disinfection applications and graphene antibacterial activity in the last ten years, respectively. Graphene family nanomaterials (GFNs) have emerged as effective antibacterial agents. The current innovations in graphene-, graphene oxide (GO)-, reduced graphene oxide (rGO)-, and graphene quantum dot (GQD)-based nanocomposites for water disinfection, including their functionalization with semiconductor photocatalysts and metal and metal oxide nanoparticles, have been thoroughly discussed in this review. Furthermore, their novel application in the fabrication of 3D porous hydrogels, thin films, and membranes has been emphasized. The physicochemical and structural properties affecting their antibacterial efficiency, such as sheet size, layer number, shape, edges, smoothness/roughness, arrangement mode, aggregation, dispersibility, and surface functionalization have been highlighted. The various mechanisms involved in GFN antibacterial action have been reviewed, including the mechanisms of membrane stress, ROS-dependent and -independent oxidative stress, cell wrapping/trapping, charge transfer, and interaction with cellular components. For safe applications, the potential biosafety and biocompatibility of GFNs in aquatic environments are emphasized. Finally, the current limitations and future perspectives are discussed. This review may provide ideas for developing efficient and practical solutions using graphene-, GO-, rGO-, and GQD-based nanocomposites in water disinfection by rationally employing their unique properties.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the various types of carbon nanoallotropes, including 0D (e.g., onion-like carbon, fullerenes, carbon and graphene dots), 1D (e.g., single-and multi-walled CNTs and carbon nanohorns), 2D (e.g., graphene), and 3D (e.g., graphite and multi-layered graphene).

(a) A preliminary proposed mechanism for the photocatalytic disinfection of Ag–AgBr/BiVO4/GA nanocomposite under visible light, in which e‒ in the VB positions of BiVO4 and AgBr are excited to the CB positions, generating e‒ and h+. The produced e‒ and h+ react with O2 or H2O and produce O2 •− and •OH, causing bacterial inactivation. (b) A scheme for the photocatalytic disinfection mechanism of Ag2S/Fe3O4/AgVO3@GM nanocomposite photocatalyst under visible light illumination, in which Ag2S and AgVO3 produce electron-hole pairs and part of the generated e‒ are transferred to rGO surface, promoting charge carrier separation. Fe3+ in Fe3O4 are reduced to Fe2+ by capturing the e‒ from AgVO3 and reacting with O2 to produce Fe3+ and O2 •−. At the same time, the photoexcited e‒ in Ag2S reduce O2 to O2 •− and the produced h+ from the VB of Ag2S oxidize organic pollutants and inactivate bacterial cells, (c) The antibacterial efficiency of thin-film nanocomposite (TFN)-GO membrane and ZIF-8/GO thin film nanocomposite (TFN-ZG) membrane. The count of E. coli was significantly reduced using ZIF-8/GO nanocomposite as a result of the synergistic effect between GO and ZIF-8, which resulted in the release of Zn2+, leading to generation of ROS, disruption of plasma membrane, and eventually bacterial inactivation.

(a) Schematic illustration for the underlying photocatalytic disinfection mechanism of the Bi2WO6/TiO2/rGO ternary nanocomposite against E. coli, in which Bi2WO6 is excited under visible light to produce e‒ and h+ on the CB and VB, respectively. Separation of the photogenerated e‒ and h+ is promoted by their transfer between TiO2 and Bi2WO6. rGO had excellent e‒ mobility, which further inhibited electron-hole pair recombination, resulting in improving the disinfection activity of Bi2WO6/TiO2/rGO towards E. coli, (b) An illustration of the hydrothermal synthesis of Ag/rGO hydrogel using the precursors; glucose, AgNO3, and GO nanosheets, as well as its use as a bactericidal filter for water disinfection applications, (c) Photocatalytic disinfection mechanism by TiO2-rGO (TR), in which the transfer of e‒ from the photo-excited TiO2 to rGO and then to O2 molecules was hindered, (d) photocatalytic disinfection mechanism by TiO2-CDs-co-decorated rGO (CTR), in which the e‒ transfer process is enhanced in the presence of CDs by acting as e‒ acceptors (for e‒ shuttling) and e‒ donors (for O2 reduction) under solar irradiation.

SEM images of E. coli (a–d) and S. aureus (e–h), untreated (a, e); and treated without any catalyst under visible light irradiation for 1h (b, f); with 200 μg/mL of CDs/rGO/P25 nanocomposite in the dark for 1 h (c, g); and with 200 μg/mL CDs/rGO/P25 nanocomposite under light irradiation for 1h (d, h). Reproduced with permission from Ref. Zhu et al. (2021). Copyright 2021, Elsevier.

(a) Small-sized GO sheets have a higher edge density and thus a stronger cutting effect; (b) whereas large-sized GO sheets have a wider lateral dimension and thus a higher potential for bacterial cells wrapping.

(a) An illustration of the membrane stress- and ROS-dependent oxidative stress-dependent antibacterial mechanism of GFNs. For membrane stress, GFNs destroy bacterial cells by piercing and extracting phospholipids from the membranes, resulting in loss of cell integrity. For ROS-dependent oxidative stress, different reactive species are produced by GFNs, such as •OH, H2O2, and O2 •−, which impair the functioning of essential biomolecules, involving proteins and nucleic acids as well as lipid peroxidation, leading to cell disintegration, (b) Illustration of wrapping/encasing and isolation of bacterial cells by GFNs and inhibiting of their growth by preventing nutrient access.
Water disinfection using graphene-, GO-, and rGO-based nanophotocatalysts.

row.entry: The LC50 of Cd Zn decreased from 0.017 to 0.001 and from 0.22 to 0.12 mg/L after combination with GO after 96h
The acute toxicity of these trace elements increased after combination with GO, GO was non- toxic to shrimp even at the highest tested concentration (5.0 mg/L)

Three different GO sizes were tested (50–200 nm, < 500 nm, and >500 nm), toxicity was dependent on GO size and concentration
GO accumulated in the heart, eyes, yolk sac, and blood vessels
The EC50 (44.3 mg/L) and the LC50 (45.4 mg/L) after 72h

GO accumulated in the gut and prompted oxidative stress, however D. daphnia released all the GO within 24h after transferring to clean water, which signified that its potential to relieve GO acute toxicity by depuration
No obvious acute toxicity was observed upon exposure of zebrafish to 1, 5, 10 or 50 mg/L GO for 14d, however some cellular alterations were identified
GO had acute and chronic effects on C. dubia, chronic exposure caused a sharp decrease in the neonate number, the feeding rate also decreased and sub-lethal GO concentrations increased ROS generation
GO with and without humic acid

Scenedesmus obliquus (green algae) and D. magna
(planktonic crustacean)
LC50 for 72h and 48 h were 20.6 and 84.2 mg/L, respectively
GO displayed significant toxicity to S. obliquus than D. magna, the presence of humic acid reduced the toxicity of GO by 28.6% and 32.3% for S. obliquus and D. magna, respectively
A remarkable reduction in gut microbiota was observed by graphene, the three tested GFNs caused significant taxa alteration and intestinal damage

The antioxidant system was affected by graphene, occurrence of pathological damage in different tissues except the intestine, brain and gills were the main target organs affected by graphene, toxicity was dependent on the organ type
No developmental or acute toxicity was observed, occurrence of variable effects that were dependent on size and shape of the nanocomposites
rGO nanocomposites (e.g., rGO-Au, rGOAg, rGO-Pd, rGO-Fe3O4, rGO-Co3O4, rGO-SnO2)

C. reinhardtii was more sensitive to rGO nanocomposites than S. obliquus, algal surface properties had a significant role in toxicity evaluation, cells having abundant functional groups and hydrophobic surfaces exhibited more interactions with the nanocomposites

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Antimicrobial nanomaterials provide numerous opportunities for the synthesis of next-generation sustainable water disinfectants. Using the keywords graphene and water disinfection and graphene antibacterial activity, a detailed search of the Scopus database yielded 198 and 1433 studies on using graphene for water disinfection applications and graphene antibacterial activity in the last ten years, respectively. Graphene family nanomaterials (GFNs) have emerged as effective antibacterial agents. The current innovations in graphene-, graphene oxide (GO)-, reduced graphene oxide (rGO)-, and graphene quantum dot (GQD)-based nanocomposites for water disinfection, including their functionalization with semiconductor photocatalysts and metal and metal oxide nanoparticles, have been thoroughly discussed in this review. Furthermore, their novel application in the fabrication of 3D porous hydrogels, thin films, and membranes has been emphasized. The physicochemical and structural properties affecting their antibacterial efficiency, such as sheet size, layer number, shape, edges, smoothness/roughness, arrangement mode, aggregation, dispersibility, and surface functionalization have been highlighted. The various mechanisms involved in GFN antibacterial action have been reviewed, including the mechanisms of membrane stress, ROS-dependent and -independent oxidative stress, cell wrapping/trapping, charge transfer, and interaction with cellular components. For safe applications, the potential biosafety and biocompatibility of GFNs in aquatic environments are emphasized. Finally, the current limitations and future perspectives are discussed. This review may provide ideas for developing efficient and practical solutions using graphene-, GO-, rGO-, and GQD-based nanocomposites in water disinfection by rationally employing their unique properties.

ce:section.ce:para: Waterborne diseases have become a threatening worldwide issue, and the need for clean drinking water has increased as a result of overpopulation and urbanization (Overgaard et al., 2021). Water contamination can be physical (e.g., suspended particles), chemical (e.g., dyes, pesticides, heavy metals, pharmaceutical residues, nitrates, chlorides), or biological (e.g., bacteria, fungi, viruses, protozoa), resulting in a deterioration in drinking water quality (Das et al., 2017; Abu Elella et al., 2021a, 2021b). Water pollution can occur as a result of contaminants leaching from municipal solid wastes, sewage treatment plant effluents, industrial effluents, and poor sanitation systems (Ghasemi et al., 2020). Almost 1.2 billion people are affected by a lack of safe and clean drinking water, resulting in several water-borne diseases spread by contaminated water (You et al., 2019). Annual diarrheal deaths are caused by waterborne pathogenic bacteria and enteric viruses, and there are increasing concerns about epidemic outbreaks caused by multidrug-resistant (MDR) bacteria (Fan et al., 2021). Water disinfection processes depend on inactivating pathogens to provide safe drinking water that is free of microbial contamination (Zeng et al., 2017a). Several water disinfection processes have been developed, including chlorination, ozonation, ultraviolet (UV) disinfection, filtration, and heat sterilization. Despite their high efficiency in inactivating pathogenic microorganisms, these disinfection methods are chemical- or energy-intensive, causing secondary pollution or contributing to global warming. Chemical disinfectants, such as chloramines and free chlorine, which are commonly used in the water industry, can react with natural water elements, producing carcinogenic disinfection byproducts (DBPs) (Srivastav et al., 2020). Although ozonation yields fewer DBPs than chemical disinfection, the reaction of ozone with bromide ions in water can produce harmful bromate ions. UV water treatment is not long-lasting and provides no additional protection against reinfection (Srivastav et al., 2020). Traditional water disinfection methods are currently considered unsuitable for long-term sustainable development (Wang et al., 2017). As a result, it is critical to develop effective, low-cost, and environmentally friendly water disinfectants that can improve the durability, stability, and reproducibility of the disinfection processes while preventing the formation of DBPs.

Recently, the application of nanotechnology in environmental remediation has received significant attention. Nanostructured materials are excellent candidates for water purification and disinfection due to their unique physicochemical and surface features. Nanomaterials (NMs) can be used as water disinfectants or integrated with cutting-edge innovations for the inactivation of water-borne pathogenic microbes. The nature of the microorganism, as well as the NM size, shape, and physicochemical properties, determine the type of interaction (Anand et al., 2019). Semiconductor photocatalysis, which is based on advanced oxidation processes, has recently emerged as a green technology for environmental remediation (Rokicka-Konieczna et al., 2019). Photocatalytic materials inactivate waterborne pathogenic microbes via a photocatalytic redox mechanism through photo-excitation under visible or near infra-red light, or UV (He et al., 2021). Electrons (e‒) in the semiconductor's valence band (VB) are excited to the conduction band (CB), generating holes (h+) in the VB. The produced e‒ and h+ contribute to the redox reaction cascade. Solar disinfection can be used to inactivate microorganisms through sunlight; however, such treatment is inefficient, particularly in large-scale applications because UV light comprises 4–5% of the entire solar spectrum and regrowth of waterborne microbes can occur (You et al., 2019; Xia et al., 2022). The development of visible light-active photocatalysts to combat water contamination is critical, given that visible light accounts for 44% of the total solar spectrum. Nanostructured photocatalysts have greater photocatalytic activity than bulk materials as a result of their large surface area and aspect ratio (i.e., length to width ratio). Moreover, they have several potential advantages, involving superb light absorption, high photostability, and effective charge separation. Photocatalytic materials inactivate waterborne microbes by producing photon-excited reactive species that are detrimental to microbial cells (Yan et al., 2021). However, the generated charges within the photocatalysts may recombine before being transferred to microbes, which causes more than a 90% reduction in the photocatalytic disinfection efficiency. As a result, modification of conventional photocatalysts is required to prevent the recombination of photo-induced e‒ and h+ (Chou et al., 2019). Preparation of heterostructured photocatalysts and photocatalyst doping can extend light absorption and improve electron-hole separation (Zeng et al., 2017b; Ashraf et al., 2020a; He et al., 2021).

Although polymer membrane-based filtration is a widely used process for water disinfection, one of the major drawbacks of membrane-based separation processes is membrane biofouling caused by biofilm formation (Ni et al., 2021). Antibacterial agents can be integrated into a polymer membrane matrix to inhibit bacterial colonization, which can be accomplished through interfacial polymerization, blending, grafting, or dip-coating (Hou et al., 2016; Orooji et al., 2017). Although composite membranes containing nanoparticles (NPs) have a satisfactory antibacterial effect, their low interfacial affinity with polymers can cause gradual detachment of the inorganic NPs, a lack of antimicrobial potential, and secondary contamination (Orooji et al., 2018). Due to its valency, carbon can form a variety of nanoallotropes, each with a distinct shape and properties, including (i) graphite, a two-dimensional (2D) material with bonded carbon atoms in a hexagonal lattice; (ii) graphene, a single graphite sheet; and (iii) fullerenes, three-dimensional (3D) cylindrical hollow structures consisting of hexagonally arranged carbon atoms, which can be spherical (C60), ellipsoidal (C70, C84), or tubular (carbon nanotubes, CNTs) (Fig. 1 ) (Szunerits and Boukherroub, 2016). Carbon-based NMs can act as antibacterial agents that can be easily combined with polymeric materials due to their interfacial compatibility, resulting in high water flux, improved salt rejection, and bacterial anti-adhesion (Chen et al., 2017; Orooji et al., 2020).

Graphene family nanomaterials (GFNs) are more advantageous than other carbon-based NMs, such as CNTs or fullerenes because of the absence of free residual metallic impurities and low mammalian cytotoxicity (Azizi-Lalabadi et al., 2020). GFNs, involving pristine graphene, GO, rGO, and GQDs have remarkable physicochemical characteristics and distinct morphological structures that can be manipulated to disinfect pathogens. GFNs are broad-spectrum antimicrobial agents capable of inactivating and controlling a wide variety of pathogenic microorganisms (Zou et al., 2016). GFNs inhibit bacterial growth via respiratory chain impairment, physical interaction with the cell membrane, energy metabolism inhibition, and cell membrane disruption (Díez-Pascual, 2020). They have unique properties, such as superior electrical conductivity, inherent mechanical strength, lightweight, specific surface area, and high thermal conductivity (Zhang and Tremblay, 2020). GO and rGO are characterized by their high surface-to-volume ratios and are characterized by the abundance of functional groups on their surfaces (i.e., hydroxyl –OH, epoxy
Image 1 , and carboxyl –COOH groups) as well as their super elasticity. They can be easily synthesized using simple techniques and at a lower cost than other carbonaceous NMs such as CNTs (ElMekawy et al., 2017). Membranes incorporating GO display high water permeability and antifouling properties due to the negative charge, surface roughness, and high hydrophilicity. The high potential for surface chemical modifications of GO and rGO, and the presence of oxygen functionalities make them perfect candidates for metal ion adsorption (Sitko et al., 2019). High adsorption capacity and selectivity can be attained by covalent/non-covalent chemical functionalization with specific functional groups, such as nitrogen, sulphur, and phosphorous (Majdoub et al., 2021; Ibrahim et al., 2022). Some organic pollutants have abundant cationic atoms and aromatic rings in their chemical structure, which enables their adsorption on the surface of GO or rGO via π−π stacking and ionic interactions (Niu et al., 2014). Moreover, GFNs can be functionalized or combined with other materials to produce composites for various applications (Gupta et al., 2018; Goda et al., 2021a, 2021b; Goda et al., 2022). For instance, GO-based nanocomposites displayed high efficiency for the removal of several water contaminants, such as pharmaceutical products (e.g., methadone and phenazopyridine residues) (Gupta et al., 2017; Karimi-Maleh et al., 2020), heavy metals (Zhang et al., 2021), radionuclides (Lingamdinne et al., 2021), and organophosphorus pesticides (e.g., parathion) (Ashraf et al., 2020b). Controllable assembly of GFNs in thin films, 3D hydrogel systems, and hybrid membranes has been accomplished (Li and Shi, 2014; Hegab and Zou, 2015; Zhu et al., 2017). Their porous architecture, physicochemical, and surface characteristics, such as degree of oxidation, surface functionality, thickness, defect density, and edge length and size can be monitored depending on the synthesis techniques used (Liu and Speranza, 2021).

This review delves deeply into the promising role of GFNs as water disinfectants against water-borne pathogenic bacteria, a critical sector for global sustainability. Despite the publication of detailed and comprehensive reviews on the antibacterial roles of GFNs (Zhang and Tremblay, 2020) and their promising application in water treatment and disinfection (Zeng et al., 2017a), an updated critical review is required to address the recent developments of using GFNs in water disinfection. Furthermore, despite their widespread production, little attention is paid to the toxicity issues raised by the discharge of GFNs into the environment. This review can achieve two goals; it can satisfy the scientific curiosity of newcomers to this emerging frontier and it can serve as an updated reference for experienced scientists in this field, taking advantage of the exceptional potential of GFNs to disinfect water contaminated with pathogenic bacteria. In addition, this review will enlighten researchers about the antibacterial role of GFNs, the physico-chemical properties that may affect the antibacterial mechanisms, as well as the toxicity concerns for the safe application of GFNs.

This review provides a comprehensive overview of GFNs, with an emphasis on water disinfection, ranging from single-component graphene materials to nanocomposites with tailored antibacterial efficiency. It begins with a brief overview of the properties and preparation methods of GFNs, involving graphene, GO, rGO, and GQDs. The potential use of graphene, GO, rGO, and GQD-based nanocomposites for water disinfection is discussed by demonstrating their functionalization with metal, metal oxide, polymers, and semiconductor photocatalysts. Water disinfection using GFNs as thin films, membranes, and hydrogels is elucidated. The effects of the physicochemical and structural properties on the antibacterial efficiency of GFNs are described, including the size, layer number, shape, edge density and length, smoothness/roughness, arrangement mode, surface functionalization, and dispersion potential of graphene nanosheets. This review emphasizes the underlying antibacterial mechanisms of GFNs. Most importantly, the biocompatibility and biosafety of GFNs on aquatic organisms are overviewed for safe water disinfection application. Furthermore, the major challenges and future perspectives for using graphene-, GO-, rGO-, and GQD-based nanocomposites in water disinfection applications are discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene family NMs: properties and preparation methods

ce:section.ce:para: Geim and Novoslov performed a ground-breaking experiment at Manchester University in 2004, in which a graphene monolayer was created using the “Scotch tape” technique (Novoselov et al., 2004). In 2010, Geim and Novoslov were awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics for their work on graphene. Since then, graphene has been in the forefront of many technological advancements, with a long list of applications exploiting the excellent properties of this “wonder material”. Graphene is a carbon nanoallotrope and is the thinnest 2D nanomaterial explored thus far (Díez-Pascual, 2020). It consists of tightly packed carbon atoms in a single-layer sheet, with benzene hexatomic rings as the basic structure. The 2D hexagonal structure of graphene resembles a honeycomb pattern with sp2 hybridization (Walimbe and Chaudhari, 2019). The unique hexagonal arrangement provides graphene with distinctive chemical, physical, and mechanical properties, including high mechanical strength, stiffness, intrinsic mobility, unparalleled thermal and electrical conductivity, ultimate tensile strength, electronic flexibility, high Young's modulus, large surface area, desirable elastic properties, optical transmission, and quantum Hall effects (Iqbal et al., 2020). Moreover, graphene has an exceptional lamellar structure, which endows graphene with flame retardancy and gas impermeability due to the effect of a tortuous flow path; hence, it can act as a gas barrier (Goda et al., 2021c, 2021d). It is the building block for the other graphitic-based NMs, and under the appropriate conditions, it is possible to convert one structure to another. It is one of the most attractive sheet-like materials and can be warped, curled, or stacked to produce 0D fullerenes, 1D CNTs, or 3D graphite, respectively (Li et al., 2021).

Despite its desirable properties, graphene has some drawbacks that need to be addressed. Graphene poor dispersion and its irreversible aggregation pose a problem that limits its future applications, which occur as a result of its low water dispersion, high surface energy, and large surface area (Yang et al., 2014). Low dispersion of graphene in composites is attributed to Van der Waals forces and π–π stacking interactions between graphene lamellae (Ghodrati and Ghomashchi, 2019). Moreover, graphene low dispersibility can be ascribed to its strong hydrophobic backbone and lack of surface functionalities, as well as the weak attraction forces between the graphitic carbon atoms and solvent molecules (Makvandi et al., 2020). Other limitations of graphene include its low absorptivity and zero bandgap (Tian et al., 2018). To overcome these drawbacks, chemical modifications have been conducted to produce graphene-derived NMs, such as GO and rGO (Fig. S1), supplementary materials. Recently, significant efforts have been made to improve graphene dispersion for optimal performance. Three main approaches can improve graphene dispersibility; involving physical dispersion, covalent bonding, and non-covalent bonding. Enhancement of graphene dispersion can be attained via the physical dispersion of agglomerated graphene nanosheets through mechanical techniques, which can lead to the formation of graphene nanosheets with fewer flakes. Physical dispersion techniques involve ball-milling, stirring, and ultrasonic treatment (Liang et al., 2018). The cost of physical methods is relatively low and they can be used for large-scale production (Leon et al., 2014); however, defects in graphene structure can take place and the dispersion rate is low. Defects in graphene structure caused by physical dispersion methods are difficult to handle and obtaining a high graphene content is difficult. As a result, physical methods are frequently used with other methods. Covalent bonding methods can be divided into polymer and small organic molecule bonding based on the material being combined with graphene (Gong et al., 2015; He et al., 2016). These methods improve graphene processability, however, they can cause defects and alter its physico-chemical properties. Non-covalent bonding methods can be divided into ionic bonding (Ahadian et al., 2015), hydrogen bonding (Liu et al., 2015), and π–π bonding (Parviz et al., 2016). They have several advantages, including simple operation, mild conditions, and low defects in graphene structure; however, other components (e.g., surfactants) can be introduced to graphene during synthesis. When developing methods for graphene dispersion, several critical points should be considered, including (i) preparation of high-quality graphene by minimizing processing effects; (ii) improvement of method controllability; (iii) removal of undesirable components to restore the superior properties of graphene; and (iv) cost-reduction for large-scale graphene production (Liang et al., 2018).

Two main approaches can be followed for the synthesis of graphene, involving (i) bottom-up and (ii) top-down approaches. The bottom-up approach depends on the assembly of graphene layers from small carbonaceous elements. The most common techniques based on this approach are chemical vapor deposition (CVD), pyrolysis, epitaxial growth, etc. (Qing et al., 2019; Neumann et al., 2019; Díez-Pascual, 2021). To allow deposition onto a substrate, all bottom-up approaches require gaseous precursors. A vacuum is required for CVD so that reactants can reach the substrate without interference. The vacuum condition is not required for epitaxial growth and pyrolysis, which makes the synthesis process feasible by lowering manufacturing costs (Yan et al., 2020). The substrate for each method is quite different. A transition metal, glass, and silicon carbide substrates are required for CVD, spray pyrolysis, and epitaxial growth, respectively. Top-down approaches, on the other hand, involve reduction and exfoliation via chemical, electrochemical, thermal, and mechanical routes (Yan et al., 2020). While scaling up is relatively easy using top-down approach, several challenges, such as low production yields and poor conductivity, limit graphene synthesis using this approach. For graphene and its products to be commercialized in industrial applications, a special emphasis should be directed towards quality, expenses, safety, reproducibility, and processability (Lin et al., 2019). With the progression from lab to pilot scale, and then to large industrial scale, new issues emerge. The synthesis parameters and processing facilities can have a direct impact on the structural properties of graphene, which can lead to low controllability at an industrial scale (Lin et al., 2019). Improving graphene products’ quality control is critical. According to a recent study, the real content of graphene in the final products is less than 50% and the content of sp 2 bonds is less than 60%, with a high level of contamination that could be detrimental for future applications (Kauling et al., 2018). More attention is needed to ensure the reproducibility and uniform mass production of graphene nanoflakes. Raw graphite materials derived from various sources typically have varying morphological features and sometimes are contaminated, affecting the quality and intrinsic properties of the final product. At the industrial scale, the safe and environmentally friendly production, storage, transportation, and application of graphene nanoflakes is critical (Zhu et al., 2018).

When graphene is modified by oxygen-containing functional groups using strong oxidizing agents, GO, a highly oxidized form of graphene, is formed. This modification disrupts the highly conjugated structure of graphene, resulting in the formation of GO nanosheets with improved chemical stability and water solubility (Marcano et al., 2018). GO has a high specific surface area similar to that of graphene, which can harbor proteins, growth factors, and hydrophilic/hydrophobic drugs. The presence of functional groups in GO, such as (―COOH) and carbonyl (–CO) groups facilitates interactions with various solvents and molecules via π–π stacking, as well as covalent, electrostatic, and hydrogen bonding (Lee et al., 2019). The presence of oxygen-containing functionalities helps to form stable GO dispersions in aqueous and organic solvents (Liu and Speranza, 2021). GO exhibits exceptional fluorescence quenching ability, surface functionality, aqueous appearance, amphiphilicity, and surface-enhanced Raman scattering properties (Sontakke and Purkait, 2021). Its outstanding properties are due to the small sp2- and sp3-bonded carbon atoms that are interconnected with hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups (Nizami et al., 2020). Hummer's method, which is widely used to synthesize GO, is based on the oxidation of graphite using sulfuric acid and potassium permanganate (Priyadarsini et al., 2018). Solvent exfoliation combined with sonication yields graphite salts, which act as precursors for GO, whereas thermal and chemical reduction of GO yields a graphene analog (Park and Ruoff, 2009). The preparation of GO nanocomposites and their synthesis routes are summarized in Fig. S2 (a, b), supplementary materials. Graphite oxidation was first reported by Brodie using KClO3 and HNO3 (Brodie, 1859). This approach was further improved by Staudenmaier in 1898 (Staudenmaier, 1898) via the slow addition of chlorate in presence of a mixture of H2SO4 and HNO3. Then, in 1958, Hummer and coworkers used a reaction mixture that included KMnO4, NaNO3, and H2SO4 (Hummers Jr and Offeman, 1958). The advantages and disadvantages of the three aforementioned methods were analyzed by Boehm and Scholz, who concluded that Hummer's method enabled the feasibility of a high O/C ratio with a carbon content of (∼47%) (Scholz and Boehm, 1969). Since then, researchers have modified the primary oxidation methods to improve the yield and oxidation degree of GO and accelerate the oxidation process.

rGO is characterized by biocompatibility and high reactivity due to the presence of several electroactive sites and a large surface area (Báez et al., 2017). Moreover, it shares structural similarities with pristine graphene in terms of surface area; strength; and electrical, thermal, and mechanical properties. Like GO, it has moderate water dispersibility (Chaudhary et al., 2021). rGO is formed by reducing the oxygen content of GO via chemical, thermal, electrochemical, or biological methods (Priyadarsini et al., 2018). It can be prepared by an abundant array of reduction methods, involving microwave-assisted reduction (Voiry et al., 2016), thermal reduction (Saleem et al., 2018), photothermal reduction (Zhang et al., 2018a), photochemical reduction (Peregrino et al., 2020), chemical reduction (Kurian, 2021), and electrochemical reduction (Pal et al., 2021) methods. Several parameters can significantly affect the chemical and physical properties of rGO derived from GO reduction. For instance, fine-tuning the oxidation degree can control electrical conductivity and electronic band-gap (Farjadian et al., 2020). Hydrazine is the most commonly used reducing agent for GO reduction. Other reductants include ascorbic acid (Abdolhosseinzadeh et al., 2015), sodium borohydride and sodium molybdate (Hu et al., 2021), and hydroquinone (Xia et al., 2021). The presence of impurities in the final product, which can be difficult to remove, is the main disadvantage of using chemical agents for GO reduction. Nonetheless, it is a favorable method for large-scale applications due to its high yield. Alternately, rGO can be physically produced by thermally reducing GO at high temperatures and exfoliating its stacked structure by extruding CO2 gas to create enough pressure to separate the stacked structures (Gholampour et al., 2017). However, thermal reduction causes structural defects on the surface of GO, affecting its electronic properties (Klemeyer et al., 2021). The electrochemical reduction of GO to rGO avoids the use of potentially hazardous chemical reductants (Renteria et al., 2020), but its main disadvantage is its limited scalability. Under alkaline conditions, a “green” reduction of GO to rGO can be achieved (Queiroz et al., 2021). The reversible deoxygenation of GO in the presence of alkaline agents is the underlying principle for green reduction, allowing the negative effects of chemical reductants to be mitigated. Amino acids (Espinoza et al., 2021), melatonin (Agarwal and Zetterlund, 2020), reducing sugars (Torres et al., 2021), bacteria (Xu et al., 2021), and plant-derived phytochemicals (Ismail, 2019) can act as green reductants for the synthesis of rGO.

In 2008, Ponomarenko and Geim fabricated GQDs (Ponomarenko et al., 2008), which are 0D carbonaceous fluorescent NMs with a graphene lattice structure. GQDs are composed of one or more layers (up to 10 layers) of graphene or rGO with lateral dimensions of less than 30 nm (Tang et al., 2012). GQDs have the same hexagonal crystalline structure as graphene with an in-plane lattice spacing of 0.24 nm (Sheely et al., 2021). GQDs have excellent optical–electrical and biological features, as well as high photostability (Wani et al., 2020). The hydrogen and oxygen content in GQDs is primarily due to the presence of –OH, epoxy, methoxy (–OCH3), –CO, and –COOH groups (Hu et al., 2013). The variations in the bandgap energy of GQDs (0–6 eV) are dependent on the degree of oxidation (Zhu et al., 2012), surface functionalization (Cho et al., 2015), and heteroatom doping (Yang et al., 2017). GQDs, unlike carbon dots (CDs), have a graphene lattice within the dots that is less than 100 nm in size and 10 layers thick (Zhu et al., 2015). GQDs have several advantages over traditional semiconductor QDs, including stable fluorescence properties (Carrasco et al., 2016), low toxicity (Zhu et al., 2012), excellent biocompatibility (Baker and Baker, 2010), and good water solubility (Kumar et al., 2015). The fluorescence properties of GQDs are dependent on their size, doped atoms, nature of surface functionalities, and excitation wavelength (Chen et al., 2018). Several methods can be used for the synthesis of GQDs based on a top-down approach, including chemical oxidation (Wang et al., 2019a), hydrothermal (Luo et al., 2019), ultrasonic-assisted (Shukla et al., 2019), electrochemical oxidation (Li et al., 2017), CVD (Deka and Chowdhury, 2017), and pulsed laser ablation (Calabro et al., 2018), or a combination of the above methods (Zhuo et al., 2012). Whilst, bottom-up approaches used for the preparation of GQDs include the microwave method (Li et al., 2018), molecular carbonization (Naik et al., 2017), and electron beam irradiation (Tian et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water disinfection using graphene, GO, and rGO-based antibacterial nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Several aspects should be considered when developing nanocomposites for use in water disinfection, including (i) the features and/or impacts of the constituent NMs in the nanocomposite, (ii) the underlying mechanisms behind the potential disinfection driven by the nanocomposite, and (iii) the integrated antibacterial efficiency of the nanocomposite. Comparative studies have been conducted using a series of designed experiments, and possible mechanisms were presented by assessing the antibacterial potential of graphene, GO, rGO, and GQD-based antibacterial nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water disinfection using graphene-based antibacterial nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic water disinfection is one of the most widely used environmentally friendly method for water and wastewater treatment (Loeb et al., 2018). During photocatalytic water disinfection, photogenerated (e‒) and holes (h+) are transferred to the surface of a photocatalyst (Dutta et al., 2019), where O2 molecules react with e‒ and generate superoxide anion radicals (O2 •−). Moreover, hydroxyl radicals (•OH) are generated either by the surface OH groups or adsorbed H2O molecules, leading to hole capture (Monteiro et al., 2015). The produced reactive species attack bacteria, causing cell membrane damage, leakage of cytoplasmic constituents, and cell death. Metal doping and sensitization are effective methods for increasing visible light photoactivity of photocatalysts (Zhang et al., 2018b; Wang et al., 2019b). The photocatalytic potential and water catalytic disinfection of a photocatalyst can be reduced by rapid electron–hole recombination (Liu et al., 2013a). Coupling graphene with photocatalysts can overcome this limitation by reducing the recombination of charge carriers (Table 1 ) (Gu et al., 2014). In one study, TiO2-graphene (TG) nanocomposite sensitized with tetrakis (4-carboxyphenyl) porphyrin (TCPP) (1 g/mL) acted as an effective photocatalyst for wastewater disinfection (Rahimi et al., 2015). The photoactivity of the TG nanocomposite for inactivating E. coli (PTCC 1330) was relatively low; however, an increase in the photo‒antibacterial activity was observed after sensitization with TCPP under visible light, resulting in a bacterial disinfection efficiency of 64%. In the prepared nanocomposite, TCPP acted as a light-harvesting agent, producing photogenerated e‒ and h+. Meanwhile, graphene inhibited charge recombination by acting as an electron acceptor, resulting in increased photocurrent density and production of more charge carriers, which had a significant role in the release of reactive species (i.e., O2 •− and •OH), causing photodamage to the E. coli cell membrane. Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), such as, PbSe (Ivanou et al., 2017), CdS (Ma et al., 2018a), CdSe (Yoo et al., 2018), CdTe (Qu et al., 2019), and PbS (Mazierski et al., 2022) have been used as effective sensitizers for TiO2 to increase the absorption range of visible light. A nanocomposite photocatalyst (CdSe QDs/graphene/TiO2) was synthesized via a hydrothermal method (Ma et al., 2018b). CdSe QDs (3 nm) were uniformly distributed on the layered TiO2/graphene nanosheets using mercaptopropionic acid as a linking agent. Under visible light illumination, the prepared ternary composite exhibited a photocatalytic antibacterial performance against E. coli cells by the dispersion of 20 mg of photocatalyst in 30 mL E. coli suspension. The antibacterial effect of the CdSe QDs/graphene/TiO2 nanosheets was attributed to the generation of h+, O2 •−, and •OH (Wang et al., 2016a). The unique orientation of the nanocomposite provided an excellent matrix for the interaction of the three components (TiO2, graphene nanosheets, and CdSe QDs), enabling the transfer of e‒ during photoexcitation. Untreated E. coli cells were rod-shaped, with no loss of cell membrane integrity; however, after treatment, the prepared nanocomposite disrupted the cell membrane integrity and caused leakage of the cytoplasmic constituents, as was observed in transmission electron microscope (TEM) images.

The use of metal oxide NPs to modify the graphene backbone is a promising approach for the effective eradication of pathogenic bacteria. Functionalized semiconductor NMs such as tin oxide (SnO2) has been extensively researched due to their shape- and size-dependent physicochemical characteristics (Pandiyan et al., 2019). The high surface-to-volume ratio of the SnO2 NPs was found to boost their inherent antibacterial potential. The bactericidal activity of SnO2 NPs is mediated by oxidative stress caused by direct contact between the bacterial cell wall and SnO2 NPs. In one study, the graphene-SnO2 nanocomposite exhibited a high bactericidal efficiency against P. aeruginosa under visible light illumination (Mohan and Manoj, 2019). Growth of P. aeruginosa was impeded at a very low concentration of graphene-SnO2 nanocomposite (250 μg/mL). In another study, P. fluorescence, S. aureus, Streptococcus enterica, Bacillus subtilis, and E. coli were inactivated using gadolinium vanadate-modified fluorine-doped graphene nanosheet (GdVO4/FG) (2 mg/mL) as an antibacterial photocatalyst under visible light illumination (Shandilya et al., 2019). The scavenging experiments showed that •OH and O2 •− had a leading role in the disinfection of the tested bacteria. Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria and bacteriophage MS2 were inactivated using 0.25 mg/mL of magnetic Fe3O4/graphene nanocomposite (G-Fe3O4) (Zhan et al., 2015). The inhibition efficiencies of S. aureus, E. faecium, and E. faecalis were 92.7%, 92.6%, and 88.5%, respectively, whereas those for E. coli, Shigella sp., and Salmonella sp. were 93.0%, 60.4%, and 82.8%, respectively. The inhibition efficiency percentage of bacteriophage MS2 by G- Fe3O4 was also high (97.8%). Furthermore, in a real water sample, the biocidal activities of G-Fe3O4 against bacteria and macrophages were 94.8% and 96.3%, respectively.

Graphene aerogel-based composite photocatalysts have been extensively studied due to their porous structure, high conductivity, and high surface area (He et al., 2018). The Z-scheme-structured nanocomposites are characterized by their high potential for preserving the highly oxidative h+ and reductive e‒ (Mamba et al., 2020). A porous 3D Z-scheme was prepared using silver–silver bromide/bismuth vanadate/graphene aerogel (Ag–AgBr/BiVO4/GA) (Lin et al., 2020). Dispersion of 20 mg of Ag–AgBr/BiVO4/GA in 30 mL of E. coli and S. aureus suspensions reduced bacterial growth by 7 log and 2.9 log, respectively (1 × 107 CFU/mL). The inactivation efficiency of the Ag–AgBr/BiVO4/GA was higher against E. coli than S. aureus, which may be ascribed to the thick cell wall of the Gram-positive bacteria (Gupta et al., 2013). A plausible photocatalytic mechanism for the inactivation of S. aureus and E. coli using Ag–AgBr/BiVO4/GA aerogel has been proposed (Fig. 2 a). When the prepared photocatalyst was illuminated by visible light, e‒ in the VB positions were excited to the CB positions of BiVO4 and AgBr to generate e‒ and h+. GA not only acted as a substrate for the two semiconductors but also served as an electro-reservoir, thus promoted the separation of electron–hole pairs. The produced h+ and O2 •− on the BiVO4 and AgBr, respectively, played a significant role during the photocatalytic reactions. A Z-scheme composite structure containing silver sulfide/ferroferric oxide/silver metavanadate graphene microtube (Ag2S/Fe3O4/AgVO3@GM) was synthesized by a hydrothermal method coupled with a capillary effect (Chen et al., 2019). In the photocatalytic bacterial inactivation experiment, 20 mg of Ag2S/Fe3O4/AgVO3@GM were dispersed in E. coli and S. aureus suspensions (20 ml) and illuminated with a 500W spherical Xe lamp. The photocatalytic disinfection mechanism of the Z-scheme Ag2S/Fe3O4/AgVO3@GM nanocomposite was proposed for elucidating the generation, separation, and transferal of electron–hole pairs (Fig. 2b). The superparamagnetic properties of Fe3O4 NPs enabled the recycling of the photocatalyst. Moreover, in the Z-scheme structure, Fe3O4 NPs acted as a redox mediator by collecting and consuming e‒ and h+ produced by Ag2S and AgVO3, thereby facilitating charge carrier separation and reactive species production. AgVO3 is an excellent candidate for bacterial inactivation and organic contaminant degradation due to its crystallization, high visible light utilization, and pertinent bandgap (Wang et al., 2018a). The prepared nanocomposite completely inhibited E. coli (100%) after 30 min under visible light illumination. The reaction process by which the bacterial cells were inactivated is depicted as follows in Eqs. (1)–(9):
