Polymer-grafted graphene-based composites provide innovative approaches for the development of antimicrobial coatings and films, which can effectively inhibit pathogenic microbes (Eskandari et al., 2019). The integration of polymeric matrices with graphene aims to improve graphene dispersion and prevent further aggregation with enhanced antimicrobial activity. This is accomplished by combining the flexibility and potential adhesiveness of a polymer matrix with graphene intrinsic properties (Tran et al., 2021). Polymeric nanocomposites are materials in which polymers and nanofillers act as matrices and reinforcing materials, respectively. Three main approaches can be used to prepare polymer-grafted graphene, involving “grafting from”, “grafting to”, and “grafting through” (Zoppe et al., 2017). The “grafting from” approach is based on surface-initiated reactions in which substrate surface is functionalized with initiator species and then polymerization starts with the anchored initiators attached to the substrate surface (Díez-Pascual, 2021). This technique eliminates any potential steric hindrance because the polymer grows directly onto the graphene surface, allowing the synthesis of nanocomposites with a high grafting extent (Zhang et al., 2018c). However, accurate monitoring of the reaction conditions and the amounts of initiators and substrates is required. Given that initiators require a functional group for grafting, GO and rGO are more commonly used than graphene (Liu et al., 2017). However, using this technique for graphene grafting can be feasible if a pre-functionalization step is performed to introduce functional groups to graphene. The most important “grafting from” techniques involve Ziegler–Natta polymerization (Dong and Liu, 2015), polycondensation (Chakraborty et al., 2018), ring-opening polymerization (Teimuri-Mofrad et al., 2019), atom transfer radical polymerization (Jin et al., 2021), and reversible addition-fragmentation chain transfer polymerization (Yildirim et al., 2021). The “grafting to” approach depends on grafting the polymer chain itself onto the substrate surface; however, functionalization steps may be required for either the polymer or substrate surface to provide reactive groups that can be employed for the coupling reaction (Díez-Pascual, 2021). In the coupling reaction, attachment of polymer chains to the substrate takes place via covalent bond formation or physisorption. In the “grafting through” approach, polymer chains are anchored on a substrate that has been modified by a double bond-containing chemical reagent.

Antibacterial and antibiofouling activities of GO-functionalized ultrafiltration membranes can be improved by surface modification using coating procedure instead of blending. Recently, an ultrafiltration membrane based on GO (200 mg/L) and polysulfone-coated with polydiacetylene displayed an antibacterial effect against E. coli MG1655 than GO-blended membrane (Cheng et al., 2019). Flattened and wrinkled cells were observed on the GO-coated membrane, implying cell viability loss and damage of the cell membrane. The coating strategy significantly increased the exposure of GO nanosheets, resulting in greater cell inactivation than the embedded GO using the blending strategy, which hampered direct contact between the nanosheets and E. coli cells. The bacterial attachment was efficiently inhibited using graphene-silicone rubber (GSR) composite elastic membrane, incorporating 0.36 wt% graphene during membrane fabrication (Jin et al., 2019). The 0.36 wt% graphene-containing membrane had the lowest pull-off force, aiding in mitigation of fouler adhesion, which could be due to the surface charge of the incorporated materials. Silicon rubber is negatively charged because it is covered with –Si–O– in water. GSR membranes had a more negative surface charge due to graphene incorporation. The presence of phospholipids, proteins, and polysaccharides in bacterial cell membrane confers a net negative surface charge (Zheng et al., 2021). Consequently, the electrostatic repulsion between bacterial cells and the GSR membrane was strong, reducing bacterial cell adhesion. Similarly, the thickness of biofilms on membrane surfaces was found to decrease with the increase in the negative surface charge (Lee et al., 2013).

Synthetic polymers are used in a wide range of applications due to their outstanding functionality, stability, strong mechanical properties, availability, and cost-effectiveness (Azizi-Lalabadi and Jafari, 2021). They are, however, non-renewable and can accumulate in the environment, causing release of CO2. Transitioning towards the use of natural polymers, such as carbohydrates (e.g., starch, chitosan, cellulose, and alginate), proteins (gelatin, wheat gluten, zein, and soy proteins), and biodegradable polymers (e.g., polylactic acid, polyethylene glycol, and polycaprolactone) reinforced with nanofillers became preferable due to the concerns raised from using artificial polymers (Li and Yang, 2021). GO-chitosan composites have been used in water treatment for the removal of dye effluents (Sabzevari et al., 2018), water disinfection (Li et al., 2019a), heavy metals (Bandara et al., 2019), and natural organic matter (Liu et al., 2021a). Highly aligned graphene/polymer nanocomposites have superior mechanical, electrical, thermal, electromagnetic, UV blocking, and gas barrier properties. The tensile strength of GO/poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) nanocomposite increased from 1.3 to 1.9 GPa after the addition of 0.5 wt% GO. Even with a low GO content, excellent UV-blocking and antibacterial properties were obtained against S. aureus (ATCC 25923) and E. coli (ATCC 25922), which was attributed to the uniform dispersion and highly aligned orientation of GO in PVA matrix fibers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water disinfection using GO-based antibacterial nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Several metal NPs such as silver NPs (AgNPs) have been used as antibacterial agents because of their high antimicrobial activity against a diverse range of pathogens (Azizi-Lalabadi et al., 2021). The bactericidal mechanism of AgNPs primarily involves (i) cell membrane damage caused by the conversion of Ag0/Ag+ with the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and (ii) disruption of intracellular metabolic pathways due to penetration of Ag+ into the cells, resulting in adverse effects (e.g., inhibition of adenosine triphosphate formation and enzyme activity) (Ahmad et al., 2020). However, there are some limitations for using AgNPs in water disinfection that should be addressed before proceeding. These limitations include dispersion, retention, and stability (Azizi-Lalabadi et al., 2021). When used in water treatment, NMs should not agglomerate in water, be easily collected and segregated, and their antibacterial potential should not be compromised. Loading metal NPs on supports such as CNTs can facilitate separation, recycling, and reuse (Kohantorabi et al., 2020); however, the apparent cytotoxicity of CNTs to mammalian cells as well as CNT surface functionalization by acid treatment have hindered their application (Saleemi et al., 2020) in addition to the high cost. GO nanosheets are excellent candidates for resolving these challenges. They exhibit bactericidal activity after direct contact with bacteria by inducing membrane stress and oxidative stress (Lu et al., 2021). In one study, an AgNP/GO nanocomposite suspension was prepared via in situ reduction and used as a micro-biocide nanocomposite to inactivate S. aureus and E. coli (Bao et al., 2011). The as-synthesized nanocomposite with a final concentration of GO (45 mg/L) had a lower bactericidal effect against S. aureus than E. coli with growth reduction of 87.6% and 100%, respectively. The lower bactericidal efficiency against S. aureus was attributed to the difference in the peptidoglycan layer thickness. The peptidoglycan layer is 20–80 and 7–8 nm for Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, respectively (Le Pape et al., 2004). The thick structure of the S. aureus cell wall might have inhibited the transfer of biocides. In another study, the GO-Ag nanocomposite had the best disinfection potential against E. coli and S. aureus compared to single-walled CNT-silver (SWCNT-Ag) and multi-walled CNT-silver (MWCNT-Ag) nanocomposites, with a disinfection rate reaching 99.9% after 25 min (Chang et al., 2016). This was accomplished by dispersing 5 mg in 25 mL of E. coli and S. aureus suspensions (105–106 CFU/mL). The high surface area of GO provided AgNPs with more coating than SWCNTs and MWCNTs. As a result, the Ag content was higher in GO-Ag than in SWCNT-Ag and MWCNT-Ag.

The antimicrobial peptide glycine-leucine amide (PGLa) and glutathione-coupled CNT were interconnected with a 3D porous GO membrane, which was highly effective for the disinfection of E. coli O157:H7 from water (Viraka Nellore et al., 2015). The synergistic effect of PGLa-CNT and 3D GO inactivated bacterial cells on the membrane, facilitated E. coli trapping, and allowed the easy binding of PGLa to E. coli cells. Additionally, a series of GO hydrogels had a high bacterial adsorption capacity (i.e., a hydrogel of 1 cm3 adsorbed 1 × 108 E. coli) (Xue et al., 2016). The bacteria entrapped in the hydrogels were then inactivated and removed from the hydrogels using a high-voltage electric pulse, allowing the recycling of the hydrogels. A novel antibacterial thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane was fabricated using zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8)/GO (TFN/ZG) via interfacial polymerization (Wang et al., 2016b). Approximately 84.3% of E. coli cells were inactivated by the synergistic effects of GO and ZIF-8. The hybrid ZIF-8/GO nanosheets showed a more intensive antibacterial effect than the other two individual materials; ZIF-8 and GO, with MIC values of 128 μg/mL for ZIF-8/GO and 256 μg/mL for both ZIF-8 and GO. Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) act as reservoirs for metal ions (e.g., Zn ions), which are known to have antimicrobial properties. Moreover, MOFs aid in the gradual release of metal ions, resulting in long-term bactericidal effects (Wyszogrodzka et al., 2016; Khan et al., 2021). When GO is not in suspension form, the primary antibacterial mechanism is oxidative stress (Tu et al., 2013). TFN/ZG triggered the production of ROS and depletion of cellular antioxidant defense systems, resulting in lipid oxidation, membrane damage, and cell death (Fig. 2c). Semi-permeable polyamide (PA) membranes are commonly used for reverse osmosis water treatment-dependent processes; however, they are susceptible to fouling, affecting water quality and quantity (Ray et al., 2015). The synergistic coupling of GO nanosheets, gold nanostars, and hydrophilic polyethylene glycol on the membrane surface resulted in a significant increase in the biofouling resistance of the PA membranes.

The presence of oxygen-containing functional groups in GOQDs increases their surface area and dispersibility and enhances their optical and electronic properties (Feng et al., 2017). The distinct chemical and physical properties of GOQDs, such as high conductivity, high electron capturing capacity, and characteristic photoluminescence, are due to the unique quantum confinement and edge effects (Pan et al., 2010), which allow electron access and diffusion as well as effective charge transfer efficiency (Sun et al., 2014). The photoluminescence up-conversion properties of GOQDs can convert absorbed long-wavelength light to short-wavelength light, increasing the light-harvesting potential of the photocatalyst (Feng et al., 2017). Furthermore, GOQDs have a peroxidase-like effect that can catalyze •OH formation by the decomposition of H2O2 (Sun et al., 2014), providing an innovative route for increasing the produced reactive species and improving the photo-oxidation potential of photocatalysts. The amount of GOQDs loaded on membrane surface should be precisely monitored to achieve maximum water permeation, antimicrobial, and antibiofouling effects in composite membranes. Surface binding of uniformly dispersed GOQDs (1 mg/mL) on polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membranes was first reported by (Zeng et al., 2016). The presence of a GOQD coating layer effectively inactivated E. coli and S. aureus cells, preventing biofilm formation on the membrane surface and exhibited antibiofouling activity. No significant improvement in antimicrobial potential and biofouling resistance was attained with further increase in GOQD concentration. Moreover, aggregation of excess GOQDs occurred, leading to formation of multi-layer particles of GOQDs on the membrane surface.

To improve the photocatalytic potential of semiconductor photocatalysts, two factors should be considered: (i) increasing the utilization of solar energy and expanding the light-harvesting region of photocatalysts from UV to the near-infrared window, which can directly enhance the photocatalytic efficiency (Wang et al., 2018b) and (ii) increasing the efficiency of photogenerated charge separation, which yields a large number of reactive species that can participate in the photocatalytic process (Zhang et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018c). Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has been extensively studied as a photocatalyst because of its cost effectiveness, high stability, non-toxicity, and good tunability (Masih et al., 2017; Kong et al., 2019; Vinoth et al., 2021). Therefore, g-C3N4 is an excellent candidate for pollutant degradation (Xu et al., 2017), water disinfection (Zhang et al., 2019a), and water splitting (Liu et al., 2021b) due to the presence of suitable positions of valence and conduction bands that are appropriate for harvesting UV and visible light (Xu et al., 2019). However, the poor utilization of Vis–NIR light, fast charge recombination, and small surface area of g-C3N4 have limited its wide application (Zhang et al., 2014a). Elemental doping improves the energy band (Jiang et al., 2018) and controls the morphological structure (Zhang et al., 2014b), whereas heterojunctions accelerate charge separation (Shi et al., 2018). In one study, oxidized nanoporous g–C3N4–decorated GOQDs (0.2 wt%) were synthesized using a facile self-assembly approach (Xu et al., 2020). Approximately 99.6% of E. coli cells were inactivated after 4h of exposure to g–C3N4–decorated GOQDs (0.2 wt%) under visible light irradiation. The GOQDs accelerated charge transfer, improved the light-harvesting potential, and increased the electron utilization by the prepared composite, which exhibited a better antibacterial photocatalytic potential against E. coli than oxidized nanoporous g-C3N4 under visible light. Enhanced antibacterial potential against E. coli and B. subtilis was observed using a photocatalytically AgNP/crumpled GO/TiO2 nanocomposite deposited onto a poly (ether sulfone) membrane (Jiang et al., 2016). Similarly, modification of a membrane filter surface with poly (N-vinyl carbazole)-GO boosted the antibacterial performance of the membrane filter against E. coli and B. subtilis with 3 and 4 log reductions, respectively (Musico et al., 2014). The antibacterial effect was attributed to ROS-mediated oxidative stress that interfered with bacterial metabolic activities, causing cell inactivation.

Photocatalytic disinfection performance of GO/vanadium pentoxide-doped with platinum (GO/V2O5/Pt) nanocomposite was assessed using Salmonella typhimurium as a model pathogenic bacterium (Mohan et al., 2020). Total organic carbon levels decreased significantly from 27.5 mg/L to 2 mg/L within 30 min, signifying the mineralization of S. typhimurium. Furthermore, K+ ions (0.8 mg/L) were released, indicating cell disintegration. The disinfection efficiency increased with an increase in the catalyst dose (100 mg/L) as a result of the absorption of more photons with increasing photocatalyst concentration. This resulted in a high disinfection potential (Guo and Wu, 2014). pH is a significant variable during photocatalytic water disinfection (Zou and He, 2016). In acidic conditions, a high photocatalytic disinfection efficiency (i.e., 9 log reduction) was achieved, whereas under neutral and alkaline conditions, the disinfection efficiencies were 6 log and 1 log, respectively. This was consistent with the findings of Schwegmann et al. (2013), wherein the maximum photocatalytic disinfection of TiO2 against E. coli corresponded to a pH of 4. In another study, the disinfection efficiency of GO-CdS composite towards B. subtilis and E. coli was assessed by the addition of 5 mg/50 mL saline solutions containing B. subtilis and E. coli (107 CFU/mL) (Gao et al., 2013b). Almost 100% of both bacteria were inhibited within 25 min under visible light. The high disinfection performance of the GO-CdS composite was due to (i) the efficient charge transfer from CdS to GO, which reduced photogenerated electron–hole pair recombination; (ii) homogenous distribution of CdS on GO nanosheets; and (iii) strong interactions between CdS and GO, which improved the stability of the prepared composite. •OH radicals, which were produced by the transfer of the photogenerated e‒ to the GO nanosheet, played a significant role in bacterial inhibition. Moreover, the photogenerated h+ that remained in the VB of CdS participated in the redox reactions that produced •OH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water disinfection using rGO-and GQD-based antibacterial nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: Composites containing several semiconductors are more advantageous than single-component systems, which are known for their low potential for visible light absorption and low photocatalytic efficiency. E. coli disinfection was achieved using Bi2WO6/TiO2/rGO ternary nanocomposite (0.5 mg/mL) prepared by a hydrothermal method (Ji et al., 2020). The radical trapping data showed that the photogenerated h+ were the main active species underlying the effective inactivation of E. coli under visible-light illumination (Fig. 3 a). In another study, Ag/rGO hydrogel composed of a controlled porous network of rGO and well-dispersed AgNPs functioned as a bactericidal filter for water disinfection (Fig. 3b) (Zeng et al., 2015). Three Ag/rGO hydrogels were prepared with varying GO concentrations (e.g., 0.5, 1, and 2 mg/mL). The Ag/rGO hydrogel inactivated approximately >97% of the E. coli cells with three different initial concentrations (103, 104, and 105 CFU/mL) within 60s, implying its high disinfection effect over different bacterial concentrations. The large pore diameter of the Ag/rGO hydrogel (>2 μm) allowed E. coli cells to pass through and closely approach the AgNPs. Cellular membrane integrity was disrupted to a greater extent with Ag/rGO hydrogel treatment than with rGO hydrogel. The concentration of Ag in the effluent was only 7.33 ± 2.08 μg/L, which was much lower than the maximum concentration of Ag (100 μg/L) recommended by the EPA for drinking water. Notably, the high retention of Ag by rGO aided in decreasing the leaching level of silver from the prepared hydrogel, which was significantly lower than the drinking water standard, implying that it is safe for use as a water purification device. Moreover, when the Ag/rGO hydrogel was used to disinfect creek and lake water samples, 94% and 99% of E. coli and coliforms were inactivated, respectively, which is practical for real-world water treatment applications. To boost the antibacterial performance of graphene-based hydrogels, a pi-conjugated molecule with five aromatic rings and two quaternary ammonium (QA) arms was assembled on the surface of the rGO hydrogel (piQA-rGO) and used as a bactericidal filter for water disinfection purposes (Wang et al., 2019c). The cytotoxicity of piQA-rGO to the two mammalian cell lines was negligible due to the absence of piQA leaching from the piQA-rGO. The prepared bactericidal filter exhibited a high antibacterial efficiency (>99.5%) against S. aureus and E. coli. Cell staining and scanning electron microscope (SEM) micrographs showed severe damage to the bacterial cell envelope, attributable to the attraction between the bacterial cells and the positively charged QA groups.

rGO coupled with titanate nanosheet were synthesized as free-standing and highly flexible hybrid films (Kim et al., 2014). The incorporation of titanate layered nanosheets improved the physicochemical properties of the rGO films by increasing mechanical robustness, surface roughness, hydrophilicity, and chemical stability. E. coli (O157:H7) cells were completely inactivated (∼100%) within 15 min using the prepared hybrid films of rGO-layered titanate. Membrane stress and oxidative stress mechanisms were involved in the high antibacterial efficiency of the prepared hybrid films. rGO co-decorated with uniformly distributed CDs and TiO2 was synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method using glucose and TiCl4 (Zeng et al., 2017b). The prepared nanocomposite exhibited a photocatalytic disinfection activity when 3 mg of the photocatalyst was added to 30 mL of the E. coli suspension buffer and stirred for 10 min at 300 rpm before light irradiation with a 300W Xe lamp (Fig. 3c). Untreated E. coli cells retained their uniform shape, intact membrane, and cell wall structure, whereas after photocatalytic disinfection, part of the E. coli cell structure faded, implying membrane disruption, which led to leakage of intracellular components. CDs boosted the reduction activity of O2 molecules, which occurred via a series of single-electron reduction reactions that were accompanied by the simultaneous production of various ROS (Sheng et al., 2013). O2 •− and H2O2 (i.e., long-lived species) were produced, and their significance was greater than that of the short-lived species (i.e., •OH) because of their longer lifetimes, allowing more contact with E. coli cells.

A nanocomposite consisting of TiO2 (P25), rGO, and multi-core@shell-clustered CDs (2% CDs/0.5% rGO/P25) was prepared via a hydrothermal method (Zhu et al., 2021). The CDs/rGO/P25 nanocomposite (200 μg/mL) exhibited the highest photocatalytic antibacterial efficiency against E. coli compared to P25, CDs/P25, and rGO/P25, which recorded 15.03, 75.63, 64.05, and 100% within 60 min, respectively, whereas S. aureus was completely inactivated within 45 min. The changes in bacterial morphology were assessed via SEM, which showed that untreated E. coli and S. aureus retained their intact cell structure (Fig. 4 a, e). Neither E. coli nor S. aureus showed any obvious morphological changes when exposed to light in the control experiment or when exposed to the CDs/rGO/P25 nanocomposite in the dark (Fig. 4 b, c, f, and g). However, after treatment of E. coli and S. aureus with CDs/rGO/P25 under visible light illumination, significant cell membrane distortion was observed (Fig. 4 d, h). Fluorescence microscopy images showed that nearly all bacterial cells were stained with propidium iodide, which can label dead bacterial cells by combining with the DNA in disrupted cell membranes, implying that the CDs/rGO/P25 exhibited high photocatalytic antibacterial properties. The produced active species (e.g., O2 •−, •OH, and h+) were capable of effectively attacking the tested pathogens. The heterojunction effectively reduced the wide bandgap of TiO2 (3.0–3.2 eV), allowing the resultant nanocomposite photocatalyst to absorb visible light, reducing photogenerated electron–hole pair recombination, and enhancing the rapid transfer of photoelectrons from the CB of TiO2 to rGO. The broad light absorption range of the CDs, as well as the close coordination with TiO2 (P25), significantly improved visible light absorption and charge carrier separation.

Polypyrrole is a conducting polymer with high conductivity, stability, and superb redox properties (Abu Elella et al., 2021c). Under visible light irradiation, it can function as a h+ transporter and an e‒ donor. Growth of E. coli was inhibited within 90 min using ternary polypyrrole protonated carbon nitride/rGO (PPy/P–C3N4/rGO) visible-light-driven nanocomposite photocatalyst (Liu et al., 2021c). The standard plate counting technique was conducted by dispersing 20 mg of PPy/P–C3N4/rGO in 49.8 mL of deionized water, followed by sonication for 10 min to ensure homogeneous dispersion. The dispersion was then mixed with 0.2 mL of E. coli stock solution for 5 min to fully disperse the bacterial cells before visible light irradiation. In p-n junction nanohybrid systems, conductive graphene can improve the separation of h+ and photogenerated e‒, enhance the absorption of visible light, and accelerate electron transfer in surface redox reactions, leading to increased ROS generation and improved photocatalytic disinfection activity.

A ternary aerogel composite was prepared using rGO (0.2 mg/mL)/silver bromide/silver aerogel (rGO/AgBr/Ag) (Xin et al., 2019). During synthesis, the 3D rGO aerogel (i) acted as a moderate reductant for AgNP synthesis and (ii) prevented AgBr NP aggregation. AgBr NPs functioned as visible light absorbers, whereas the 3D rGO aerogel and AgNPs provided an electron sink platform for electron transfer. When E. coli cells were treated using 3D rGO aerogel for 20 min under visible light illumination, no bactericidal activity was observed, indicating that it had a negligible antibacterial potential. The interconnection between the 3D rGO aerogel and AgNPs in the AgBr matrix enhanced the photoactivity and inactivated 1.0 × 106. CFU/mL E. coli cells. A series of photoelectrochemical analyses were conducted to understand the underlying mechanisms for the enhanced antibacterial photoactivity of the rGO/AgBr/Ag aerogel composite compared to that of bare AgBr. The rGO/AgBr/Ag aerogel exhibited a more depressed semicircle at a higher frequency than bare AgBr NPs, as demonstrated by electrochemical impedance spectra, indicating that the rGO/AgBr/Ag aerogel composite had a higher effective charge carrier transfer than bare AgBr. It was proposed that the rGO/AgBr/Ag aerogel composite inactivated E. coli cells as follows: (i) electron–hole pairs were formed by the photoexcitation of AgBr NPs, (ii) e‒ were easily transferred to the conduction band of rGO aerogel from that of AgBr NPs, and (iii) the AgNPs supported on the rGO trapped the accumulated e‒, improving the transfer of photogenerated e‒, and enhancing the photoactivity of the prepared composite when exposed to visible light. Simultaneously, the remaining photogenerated h+ contributed to the inactivation of E. coli cells.

E. coli cells (5 × 106 CFU/mL) were completely inactivated using lead-bismuth ferrite/rGO (0.5%) (Pb–BiFeO3/rGO) nanocomposite photocatalyst (100 mg/L) under visible light illumination for 30 min (Li et al., 2019b). TEM images revealed that untreated E. coli cells had a multi-layered cell surface that included a cytoplasmic membrane, a peptidoglycan layer, and an outer membrane (Fig. S3a), supplementary materials. E. coli cells had an irregular shape after being exposed to Pb–BiFeO3/rGO for 10 min under visible light illumination, and the cytoplasmic membrane shrank and detached from the cell wall (Fig. S3b), supplementary materials. The outer membrane was significantly damaged after 30 min of illumination, as shown in Fig. (S3c), supplementary materials. The Pb–BiFeO3/rGO photocatalyst induced the transcription of a number of oxidative stress-related genes in E. coli, including lysU, trxC, and cydB (Monrás et al., 2014). However, low expression of sucA was observed, which governs oxidative stress protection (Monrás et al., 2014). According to SEM and XRD, the morphology and structure of Pb–BiFeO3/rGO after three successive disinfection cycles resembled that of the photocatalyst used in the first cycle. E. coli was completely inactivated in the fourth disinfection cycle, but it took a longer time (i.e., 45 min), which could be due to metal ion release from the prepared photocatalyst. Furthermore, the slight reduction in the antibacterial potential could be due to the accumulation of bacterial residues on the photocatalyst surface, causing blocking pf photocatalyst active sites even after washing with deionized water and ethanol (Liang et al., 2016).

The incorporation of GQDs into the active polyamide layer of a thin-film membrane improved its hydrophilicity, biocidal activity, and forward osmosis performance (Seyedpour et al., 2018). Approximately 90–95% of S. aureus and E. coli cells were inhibited using the as-synthesized thin-film membrane. The uniform dispersion and low agglomeration of GQDs with multiple active edges increased their contact with bacterial cells on membrane surface, resulting in surface cytotoxicity. The majority of the bacterial cells attached to the as-prepared thin-film membrane lost their cell membrane integrity, exhibiting flattened or shrunken structures. Selected graphene-, GO-, and rGO-based hydrogels, aerogels, thin films, and membranes are summarized in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical and structural properties affecting the antibacterial efficiency of GFNs
Graphene sheet size and layer number
ce:section.ce:para: The size of graphene sheets is a primary factor in determining their antibacterial potential. It can be adjusted or tuned depending on the synthesis method or post-treatment (Mohammed et al., 2020). The particle size influences the dispersion and adsorption of GFNs, which are essential for the physicochemical interaction with microbes (Cai et al., 2011). In one study, the antibacterial efficiency of GO sheets towards E. coli was dependent on the lateral sheet size (Liu et al., 2012b). Large-sized GO nanosheets (40 μg/mL) had a higher potential for bacterial inactivation than small-sized nanosheets, which could be attributed to the covering of the entire cell by the large-sized GO, blocking the cell membrane active sites, inhibiting cell proliferation, and causing loss of cell integrity. In another study, GO suspensions (200 μg/mL) with surface areas ranging from 0.01 to 0.65 μm2 exhibited antibacterial efficiency against E. coli (Perreault et al., 2015). The antibacterial efficiency of the GO surface coating improved four-folds when the surface area was decreased to 0.01 μm2. The high antibacterial activity of the small-sized GO nanosheets was due to oxidative stress mechanisms as well as the high defect density of the small-sized sheets. On the other hand, increasing the sheet size led to an increase in the antibacterial potency of GO with respect to E. coli suspensions via a cell entrapment mechanism, with complete inactivation after 3h of exposure to GO (0.65 μm2-sized sheets).

Four GO nanosheet suspensions (25 μg/mL), namely GO-1, GO-2, GO-3, and GO-4, were prepared with different sizes, i.e., 1295, 2015, 3074, and 4544 nm, respectively (Yu et al., 2020). The effect of GO size on the nano-bio interaction-based antibacterial mechanism (cutting effect and cell entrapment) was investigated against Streptococcus mutans using SEM. Small sheets had a greater cutting effect, whereas large sheets had a higher cell entrapment effect (Fig. 5 a and b). When S. mutans was exposed to GO-1, significant cutting behavior and cell damage were observed, but zero or very weak cell entrapment effect. GO-2 was larger in size than GO-1 and displayed a cell entrapment effect while also exhibiting a potent cutting effect. GO-3 was larger in size than GO-1 and GO-2 and displayed a significant cell entrapment effect, with a weaker cutting behavior than GO-2. GO-4 was the largest in size and exhibited only a cell entrapment effect with almost no cutting effects. Notably, GO-2 exhibited the highest efficiency against S. mutans due to the combination of cell entrapment and cutting effects, as well as having the lowest negative charge, which reduced the electrostatic repulsive forces with the negatively charged S. mutans. The number of graphene layers is another significant factor in determining the antibacterial potency of GFNs. Increasing the layer number thickens GFNs, resulting in a weak “nanoknife” effect and a decrease in dispersibility due to increased aggregation tendency, reducing the possibility of contact between GFNs and bacteria (Zou et al., 2016). Theoretical molecular dynamics (MD) simulations revealed that three-layered graphene nanosheets had a higher energy barrier than monolayered graphene nanosheets, which prevented their penetration into the lipid bilayer, implying that graphene sheets with few layers would have a high potential to damage the bacterial cell membrane (Wang et al., 2013).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Shape, edges, smoothness/roughness, and arrangement mode of graphene sheets
ce:section.ce:para: The shape of graphene-based NMs affects their antibacterial activity (Zou et al., 2016). To elucidate graphene antibacterial activity, graphene nanofilms with varying edge lengths and orientation angles were synthesized via a liquid-phase exfoliation procedure (Pham et al., 2015). The synthesized graphene films had two different nanotopographic surfaces, termed graphene nanorough (GN-R) and graphene nanosmooth (GN-S). Almost 70% of S. aureus and P. aeruginosa were inhibited by GN-S, whereas GN-R had a lower antibacterial effect towards S. aureus (53.1%) than P. aeruginosa (87.6%), which was consistent with the fact that the degree of bacterial attachment was affected by the surface nanotopography (Ivanova et al., 2011). Theoretical simulations have shown that graphene nanosheets with sharp edges can easily penetrate the bacterial cell membrane due to their low energy barrier (Linklater et al., 2018). Wrinkled graphene surfaces have a larger surface area than planar biointerfaces, making them more sensitive matrices to interactions with biological systems (Yang et al., 2009). Highly wrinkled GO (1 mg/mL) films with nanoscale grooves exhibited significant antibacterial potential against E. coli, S. aureus, and Mycobacterium smegmatis within a short exposure time (15 min) (Zou et al., 2017). When S. aureus and E. coli were treated with GO-505 (with a surface roughness of 500 nm), their viability was reduced by 20% compared to the control samples. Treatment of M. smegmatis with GO-845 (with a surface roughness value of 850 nm) decreased its viability to approximately 30% compared to the control. The wrinkled geometrical nature of the GO films induced their adhesion to the bacterial cells, and facilitated their tapping within the wrinkles of GO. The close contact between the GO films and the cell wall resulted in membrane stress, cell membrane rupture, intracellular component leakage, and eventually cell death. The arrangement mode of the graphene nanosheets has a significant role in determining their antibacterial potency. For GO, three arrangement modes can usually be obtained; random, horizontal, and vertical. Vertically arranged GO sheets with sharp edges exhibited the highest antibacterial activity by allowing more interaction with the tested bacteria, facilitating high penetration into the bilayer membrane, and causing significant oxidative stress via electron-transfer-mediated oxidation reactions (Lu et al., 2017).

GFNs have a high tendency for agglomeration because of their high surface energies, hydrophobicity, and van der Waals forces between the graphene nanosheets (Assali et al., 2019). Agglomerated GFNs have low dispersibility and adsorption potential, which affects the blade cutting effects and decreases the frequency of microbial interactions with the GFNs (Zou et al., 2016). For instance, well-dispersed GO sheets can be used to form thin films that can easily trap bacterial cells, whereas rGO forms large aggregates if not completely exfoliated, resulting in a decrease in the antibacterial activity (Liu et al., 2011). In one study, single-layer GO nanosheets were chemically synthesized via exfoliation and chemically reduced by melatonin solution (Akhavan et al., 2011). The agglomerated sheets in the melatonin-GO-bacterial suspension (5 mg/mL) trapped E. coli, resulting in bacterial inactivation, whereas the GO suspension and melatonin solution had no significant antibacterial effects. The bacteria trapped within the aggregated sheets could not proliferate in the testing medium because they were biologically disconnected from the surrounding environment. However, the bacteria reactivated, consumed glucose, and could multiply after the removal of sheets from E. coli surface using sonication, implying that graphene nanosheets can potentially serve as encapsulating materials to inactivate microorganisms. The functionalization of the graphene sheet surface through covalent and non-covalent modifications is critical to prevent particle agglomeration, which can directly influence its antibacterial efficiency (Li et al., 2008). The oxygen-containing functional groups can alter the amphipathic and blade effects of GFNs, thereby affecting their antibacterial efficiency (Fan et al., 2014). Hydration of pristine GO (5 mg/mL) produced a high density of carbon radicals (•C) on the GO surface, which significantly improved the bactericidal potential against E. coli (ATCC 25922 and ATCC BAA-2452); silver-resistant E. coli strain, L. crispatus (ATCC 53545); and L. crispatus (ATCC 55221) (Li et al., 2016). Bacterial cells were inactivated as a result of GO contact with the bacterial cellular membrane, leading to lipid peroxidation with no cellular uptake.

A wide range of experimental parameters should be taken into account when assessing the antibacterial efficiency of GFNs, including the type and physicochemical properties of the antibacterial agent, bacterial shape and category, and the application environment (in vitro/in vivo) (Mohammed et al., 2020). Each microorganism can only grow under certain physicochemical conditions; therefore, identifying and understanding these conditions is critical to manipulate microbial growth in a precise manner (Zou et al., 2016). The antimicrobial efficiency of GFNs is usually dependent on the physicochemical interactions between the GFNs and pathogenic microbes. The cell envelope is the main barrier and a distinguishing parameter for the antibacterial effect of GFNs. Gram-positive bacteria have a thick cell wall composed of a 30–100 nm peptidoglycan layer, whereas Gram-negative bacteria have a thin peptidoglycan layer (5–10 nm) but contain an outer cellular membrane composed of 7.5–10 nm lipopolysaccharides. The outer cellular membrane provides bacterial cells with the required protection and stability from harsh environments via the exclusion of toxic molecules (Willdigg and Helmann, 2021).

Graphene nanosheets can pierce the bacterial cellular membrane with their sharp edges, resulting in pore formation, changes in bacterial osmotic pressure, and cell death (Pham et al., 2015). The sharp edges are referred to as nanoknives, blades, or cutters and are one of the most crucial antibacterial mechanisms of GFNs (Henriques et al., 2018; Baek et al., 2019; Nguyen et al., 2021) Membrane stress is initiated when GFNs pierce the bacterial cell membrane with the blade-like edges, causing leakage of intracellular constituents, and cell death (Fig. 6 a). Graphene nanosheets have a relatively high surface area due to their sharp edges, making them attractive as direct-contact bactericidal agents (Properties et al., 2021). In this regard, increasing the sheet area will enhance the inactivation rate (Perreault et al., 2015). Computational simulations, such as MD, were conducted to study the underlying mechanisms of interaction between a series of graphene nanosheets and the lipid bilayer membrane (Guo et al., 2013). MD was used in one study to evaluate the interaction of single and multi-layered pristine graphene, oxidized graphene, and graphene coated with different densities of lipid molecules (Wang et al., 2013). It was observed that pristine graphene and few-layered graphene nanosheets pierced through the lipid bilayer and changed position to lay in the middle of the bilayer in a parallel orientation. The oxidized graphene nanosheet edges pierced through the lipid bilayer and remained either parallel in the middle of the bilayer or pointing upwards through the bilayer. Graphene covered with a low density of lipid molecules could also penetrate through the bilayer; however, the effect of piercing was limited when the entire graphene sheet was fully encapsulated with lipid molecules, which occurred as a result of the high energy barrier (Wang et al., 2013).

Oxidative stress can be caused by either ROS-dependent or ROS-independent mechanisms. ROS-dependent oxidative stress occurs as a result of excessive intracellular ROS accumulation, such as H2O2, O2 •−, •OH, or singlet oxygen (1O2), which causes inactivation of intracellular proteins, peroxidation of lipids, mitochondrial dysfunction, and gradual disruption of the cell membrane, leading to cell apoptosis (Fig. 6a) (Zhang and Tremblay, 2020). ROS-independent oxidative stress is based on the disruption and oxidation of a significant intracellular constituent without the formation of ROS; instead, charge transfer from the cell membrane to graphene nanosheets acts as e‒ pumps (Li et al., 2014).

In one study, GO and rGO (175 μg/mL) stimulated the production of O2 •−, which caused viability loss of P. aeruginosa due to oxidative stress and changes in the cellular redox status (Gurunathan et al., 2012). The nitroblue tetrazolium assay (NBT) revealed that ROS levels in GO-and rGO-treated P. aeruginosa were 3.8- and 2.7-folds higher than those in control cells, respectively. Similarly, treatment of E. coli and S. aureus with GO (100 μg/mL) increased the intracellular ROS levels i.e., 3.41- and 3.74-folds, respectively. Intracellular ROS levels exceeded the bacterial cell threshold for self-regulation and maintenance of cellular redox homeostasis, resulting in oxidative stress (Lukowiak et al., 2016). In another study, harmful ROS were produced when the rGO sheets were exposed to visible-light illumination (Eq. (10)–(14)) (Dutta et al., 2015). The reactions showed that 1O2 was initially produced as a result of energy transfer from the excited rGO* to triplet oxygen (3O2). •OH was also produced. The next step was the reduction of 1O2 to O2 •− by the excited rGO*.

The survival percentage of E. coli cultures treated with GO (25 μg/mL) was 68.8 9.2% (Chong et al., 2017). Interestingly, when cultures with the same GO concentration were exposed to simulated sunlight, the survival percentage was further reduced to 24.9 ± 5.9%, indicating that simulated sunlight significantly boosted the antibacterial potential of GO via ROS-independent oxidative stress. Solar irradiation accelerated electron transfer from the intracellular antioxidant defense systems of E. coli to GO, resulting in the disruption of intracellular biomolecules that can normally protect against oxidative stressors. Light-induced e‒ promoted the reduction of GO and generation of •C, which might have contributed to the bactericidal activity of GO. Bactericidal efficiency of four graphene-based materials, graphite, graphite oxide, GO, and rGO, with a concentration of (40 μg/mL) were tested against E. coli under the same incubation conditions (Liu et al., 2011). GO exhibited the best bactericidal potential, followed by rGO, graphite, and graphite oxide. The antibacterial mechanism comprised three stages; (i) initial deposition of bacterial cells on graphene-based nanosheets; (ii) direct contact between the sharp nanosheets and the bacterial cells, which caused membrane stress; and (iii) initiation of O2 •−-independent oxidation reactions.

Because graphene is the thinnest sheet material, it has remarkable flexibility, which enables it to wrap and isolate bacterial cells, causing nutrient depletion (Fig. 6b) (Mohammed et al., 2020). GO (10 μg/mL) exhibited a differential antibacterial mechanism in GO-treated Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, with cell entrapment observed primarily in S. aureus and E. faecalis, whereas membrane damage was observed in P. aeruginosa and E. coli as a result of physical contact (Pulingam et al., 2019). A nisin (antimicrobial peptide)-conjugated 3D porous GO membrane was developed to effectively separate and completely disinfect MDR-methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) pathogenic microorganisms in water (Kanchanapally et al., 2015). MRSA was trapped by the porous membrane due to size differences, enabling only water to pass through. Pathogens were successfully captured and wrapped by the prepared membrane as revealed by fluorescence microscope, TEM, and SEM images. Colony counting data showed that the developed membrane completely removed and destroyed the MRSA pathogens from the tested water sample. Notably, when the efficiency of nisin, GO membrane, and nisin-conjugated GO membrane were compared, it was found that the latter membrane could effectively destroy MRSA pathogens because of the synergism between nisin and GO as a multimodal antibacterial mechanism. This was consistent with the findings of Carpio et al. (Mejías Carpio et al., 2012), wherein the wrapping action of GO was the primary antibacterial mechanism against Bacteriodes subtilis, Rhodococcus opacus, Cupriavidus metallidurans, and E. coli.

Photosensitizers based on zinc phthalocyanine-based derivatives (ZnPc) have been extensively used in sensing applications (Korkut et al., 2016), cancer treatment (Zamani et al., 2018), antimicrobial photodynamic therapy (Gao et al., 2018), and energy harvesting (Siddique et al., 2021). A nanohybrid system containing GO-conjugated zinc phthalocyanine (ZnPc–GO) exhibited antibacterial efficiency towards E. coli without the need for photoexcitation (Das et al., 2019). Two types of ZnPc molecules were synthesized with distinct morphologies, including potato-chip-like α-phase (ZnPc(A)) and nanorod-like β-phase (ZnPc(B)). Inactivation of E. coli cells by the prepared nanohybrid was dependent on the charge transfer from the negatively charged E. coli cellular membrane to the ZnPc–GO nanohybrid. Gram-negative bacterial cells have an outer membrane containing lipopolysaccharides (LPS) and phospholipids. The net negative charge on the bacterial cell surface is due to the presence of phosphate and carboxylate functional groups in the peptidoglycan and LPS layers (Slavin et al., 2017). As a result, electron transfer from a negatively charged bacterial cellular membrane to an electro-positive or electron-drawing surface disrupts the bacterial surface potential, resulting in bacterial cell death (Li et al., 2014). A large number of E. coli cells were attached to the surface of the ZnPc(B)–GO nanohybrid, indicating that the negatively charged E. coli could interact with the surface of ZnPc(B)–GO via electrostatic attraction forces. GO nanoflakes had a potent π-π interaction with ZnPc(B), which aided in the transfer of free e‒ from GO to ZnPc(B) (Wang et al., 2015). The strong adherence of E. coli to the surface of ZnPc(B)–GO was attributed to electron-charge transfer from GO to ZnPc(B) molecules, rendering the GO surface more electron deficient and imparting a partial electro-positive charge to the GO surface. Furthermore, the sharp protruding edges of the nanorod-like ZnPc(B) facilitated interactions with the outer bacterial membrane, causing mechanical rupture of membrane phospholipids and irreversible damage to membrane integrity. However, there was no significant electronic interaction between the ZnPc(A) and GO nanoflakes.

Identifying the precise interactions between NMs and the cellular membrane can help in understanding and analyzing their cytotoxicity, which is essential for safe application. The robust hydrophobic interactions between graphene-based NMs and lipid molecules initiate a driving force that causes membrane collapse—a process known as “nanoscale dewetting” (Basu et al., 2021). GO nanosheets (100 μg/mL) destroyed E. coli inner and outer cellular membranes (Tu et al., 2013). TEM images showed that E. coli was inactivated via a three-stage process: in stage (I), E. coli tolerated low concentrations of GO sheets for a short period; in stage (II), the cellular membrane of E. coli was partially disrupted, with certain spots having low surface phospholipid density but no obvious cuts, and in stage (III), the cellular integrity of E. coli was disrupted; cellular membranes were highly deteriorated, and in some cells, the cytoplasmic constituents leaked. These representative stages elucidated the dynamic process of GO-induced cellular membrane degradation in E. coli. MD simulations identified the molecular details of the process via two types of interaction mechanisms; one for the inner membrane and one for the outer membrane. The simulations clearly revealed that the GO nanosheets penetrated the cellular membranes of E. coli cells within a few nanoseconds. During the penetration of the GO nanosheet to both membranes, three distinct modes were observed; swing, insertion, and extraction. During the swing mode, the GO nanosheet exhibited a swing (back-and-forth) motion for a few nanoseconds, making multiple contacts with the membrane surface several times. During the insertion mode, the cellular membranes trapped the GO tail end as a result of van der Waals attraction forces and hydrophobic interactions. It took only a few nanoseconds for the GO nanosheet to pierce the lipid bilayer membrane. The insertion/cutting mode was consistent with an earlier study by Akhavan and Ghaderi (2010). During extraction, phospholipids were extracted from the lipid bilayers by strong dragging forces caused by GO nanosheets. Disruptive phospholipid extraction caused membrane deformation and loss of cell membrane integrity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosafety and biocompatibility of GFNs on aquatic organisms
ce:section.ce:para: The widespread use and production of graphene-based NMs has increased the risk of unintentional exposure. However, due to the wide variation in their physicochemical properties, conflicting findings about their biocompatibility and biosafety have been reported (Table 2 ).

To shed light on the biosafety of GFNs, it is essential to assess their interactions with bio-macromolecules, such as nucleic acids and proteins (Kumar et al., 2019). Generally, the severity of the toxic effect of any nanomaterial varies according to its concentration, preparation method, and physicochemical properties. Photoautotrophic organisms such as cyanobacteria and algae play critical roles in the ecosystem, including O2 evolution, N2 and CO2 fixation, biomass production, and self-treatment of contaminated water (Brodie et al., 2017). Generally, GFNs can affect algae via four main mechanisms; shading effect, agglomeration, membrane damage, and nutrient depletion (Fig. S4), supplementary materials (Zhao et al., 2017). The toxicity effects of three types of GFNs, that is, GO, rGO, and multi-layered graphene (MG), on freshwater algae (e.g., Chlorella pyrenoidosa) was investigated (Zhao et al., 2017). The effects varied significantly depending on the physicochemical properties and colloidal behavior of the samples. The 96h median values of effective concentration for GO, rGO, and MG to C. pyrenoidosa were 37, 34, and 62 mg/L, respectively. The observed GO toxicity was attributed to the shading effect, nutrient depletion, and oxidative stress-dependent membrane damage. No shading effect on algal growth was observed by MG and rGO due to their low dispersion, whereas nutrient depletion caused 27% and 35% of the overall toxicity by MG and rGO, respectively. The effect of various GO concentrations (0.5, 1, 2.5, and 5 mg/L) on the marine algae Picochlorum sp. was studied (Hazeem et al., 2017). The lowest concentration (0.5 mg/L) had no effect on algal growth and pigment content, whereas high GO concentrations affected the growth of Picochlorum sp. and the photosynthetic pigment content. In another study, the toxicity of GO concentrations (1–50 mg/L) was studied on the cyanobacterium Microcystis aeruginosa (Tang et al., 2015). Low GO concentrations exhibited no toxic effects; however, increasing the GO concentration beyond 10 mg/L resulted in high adhesion to the cells, causing cell damage. GO exhibited phototransformation after sunlight irradiation for 25d, and the highest transformation (reduction) occurred on day 8 (Zhao et al., 2020). GO was more toxic to algal cells after phototransformation. Low molecular weight species were released and identified as aromatic compounds, which were involved in the increased toxicity.

When assessing the ecotoxicity of GO in aquatic environments, the chemical composition of GO, particularly the oxidation degree, should be considered. Three different types of GO were prepared via different synthesis techniques (i.e., Hofmann, Hummers, and Tour) to attain varying oxidation degrees (Malina et al., 2019). Considering the known antibacterial properties of GO, the cyanobacterium (Synechococcus elongatus) was more sensitive to GO toxicity than the green algae strain (Raphidocelis subcapitata). Lightly oxidized GO samples caused more damage to the integrity of the algal membrane than heavily oxidized GO. Microscopic images and flow cytometry showed that the lightly oxidized GO acted as a “nanoblade”, causing mechanical disruption to algal cells as a result of the low coverage of GO sheet edges with oxygen-containing functional groups. The extensive functionalization of the highly oxidized GO sample caused minor damage by preventing direct physical interaction with algal cell membranes. Notably, the hydrophilic potential of GO sheets facilitates their stable suspension in water, resulting in a high tendency for accumulation in aquatic organisms via ingestion, filtration, or by superficial dermal contact when found in aquatic systems. The effects of GO on aquatic organisms can also vary depending on the surrounding environmental factors, including water pH, temperature, salinity, and absence/presence of sediments.

Several studies have evaluated the toxicity of graphene and its derivatives using zebrafish (Hu et al., 2017; Jia et al., 2019). The toxicity of GO was assessed using zebrafish as an in vivo model to assess its effects on embryonic development as well as its relationship to cardiovascular defects (Bangeppagari et al., 2019). There were no effects observed on embryonic development at low concentrations (0.1–0.3 mg/mL); however high GO concentrations (0.4–1 mg/mL) caused significant embryonic mortality, increased heartbeat, delayed hatching, cardiovascular defects, cardiotoxicity, retardation of cardiac looping, decreased hemoglobinization, and increased apoptosis. In zebrafish larvae, GO (0.01–1 μg/L) induced Parkinson-like symptoms, resulting in the loss of >90% of dopamine neurons (Ren et al., 2016). The effects of graphene and GO on the behavioral dysregulation of adult zebrafish (Danio rerio) have been studied (Audira et al., 2021). Zebrafish were chronically exposed to two different doses of graphene (0.1 and 0.5 mg/L) and GO (0.1 and 1 mg/L) for 14 d. As a result of the differences in the physicochemical properties of graphene and GO, both exhibited distinct patterns in terms of altering the behavior of D. rerio. After the exposure period, all treated groups displayed altered predator avoidance behavior, and graphene-treated D. rerio also exhibited abnormalities in exploratory behavior. Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays revealed a decrease in the levels of several neurotransmitters and cortisol after exposure to a high concentration of graphene.

GFNs are excellent matrices for the development of versatile nanocomposites for water disinfection applications. This review discusses recent advances in the use of graphene-, GO-, rGO-, and GQD-based antibacterial nanocomposites for water disinfection. According to the literature, various strategies such as doping, surface sensitization, increasing electrocatalytic active sites, functional group grafting, coupling, and heterojunctions can be used to improve the disinfection potential of graphene-, GO-, rGO-, and GQD-based nanocomposites. Photocatalytic water disinfection is a promising water treatment and purification technology. Insufficient light availability and rapid electron–hole recombination by some photocatalysts limits their widespread application. The good physicochemical, optical, mechanical, and thermal characteristics of GFNs make them ideal matrices for supporting semiconductors by preventing semiconductor particle aggregation, improving light absorption and utilization, and preventing recombination of photogenerated e− and h+. Z-scheme composites containing graphene-derived nanocomposites are preferred because of their ability to preserve highly oxidative and reductive h+ and e‒; respectively. Thin-film membranes, 3D gel systems, and hybrid membranes incorporating graphene-, GO-, rGO-, and GQD-based nanocomposites displayed superior water-disinfection properties. Several physicochemical and morphological properties of GFNs influence their antibacterial activity, including size, layer number, shape, edge length and density, smoothness/roughness, arrangement mode, dispersion, and surface functionalization. Several GFN antibacterial mechanisms have been proposed, including membrane stress, ROS-dependent and ROS-independent oxidative stress, cell wrapping/trapping, charge transfer, and interactions with cellular components. The biosafety and biocompatibility of GFNs have been overviewed due to their widespread production and application. In summary, the use of graphene, GO-, rGO-, and GQD-based antibacterial nanocomposites for water disinfection is gaining traction and has made significant advances; however, extensive research in this developing field is still required to overcome the current challenges. The challenges and future prospects of using GFNs in water disinfection are listed below;
•
The production of graphene-, GO-, rGO-, and GQD-based nanocomposites with low fabrication costs and high stability and efficiency is desirable but challenging. Limited mass production is one of the major bottlenecks to their practical application in water treatment.
•
Surface design and tailoring of ordered and controllable graphene-based microstructures can significantly improve their water remediation potential.
•
The use of light-triggered GFNs for photocatalytic water disinfection is limited by increased energy consumption. Solar-based photocatalysis has been investigated as a potential alternative to reducing the energy dependence of photocatalyst-based water disinfectants; however, these disinfectants would require unrealistically high surface areas to achieve a satisfactory yield. Furthermore, the process becomes unviable in locations where sunlight is scarce or at night. The advancement of pilot-scale photoreactor design will aid in the development of a comprehensive water disinfection technology.
•
Regeneration and recycling are critical criteria for determining the stability, reusability, efficiency, and economic feasibility of graphene-, GO-, rGO-, and GQD-based nanocomposites. One of the major drawbacks that can limit the application of GFNs is the difficulty in separating them from treated water; however, this can be resolved by incorporating GFNs with magnetic properties or using them as thin-film membranes and 3D gels. This will significantly facilitate and simplify their recovery and further reuse in subsequent disinfection cycles. Long-term recycling experiments, rather than the four or five disinfection cycles commonly reported in the current studies, should be conducted to precisely determine their reusability.
•
Several research studies have shown that the chemical functionalization of graphene surfaces can result in a good interfacial interaction, increasing its environmental applicability; however, the physicochemical interaction of GFNs with other NMs is still unknown. Unraveling the nature of chemical and/or physical bonding can aid in the development of highly stable nanocomposites.
•
A combination of in vivo, in vitro, and computational simulations can aid in precisely determining the antimicrobial mechanisms of graphene-, GO-, rGO-, and GQD-based nanocomposites.
•
Identifying the nature and morphology of the bacterial cell wall is critical to improving disinfection potential. Another problem is that some laboratory research on the antibacterial potential of GFNs does not accurately reflect the real environment because many viability tests have only been conducted using pure cultures of model bacterial strains, such as P. aeruginosa, E. coli, B. subtilis, and S. aureus. As a result, real environmental conditions rather than laboratory conditions are required.
•
When investigating individual factors involved in the antibacterial action of GFNs, it is critical to control the other factors. As a result, more systematic and detailed studies on the antibacterial activities of GFNs are required. To unravel the various dynamic phenomena that occur during antibacterial mechanisms, more in-depth analysis and rational designs are required.
•
Several graphene-based hydrogels, thin membranes, free-standing films, and coatings have been developed for water disinfection. In this case, roughness and wettability are critical factors influencing bacterial adhesion. From a sustainable technological perspective, several issues should be addressed, including the development of facile and scalable methods; testing the stability of the nanocomposites, hydrogels, and membranes in practical water treatment processes; and assessing their risks to human health and the environment.
•
According to the guidelines of the World Health Organization for drinking water, the leaching of metal NPs coupled with graphene-, GO-, rGO-, and GQD-based nanocomposites in water is unsuitable for “green” water disinfection because it may pose a threat to aquatic fauna and flora and is toxic to humans. Even if no leaching of toxic metals occurs in laboratory studies, changes in water pH and alkalinity, as well as prolonged use, may result in their release. As a result, their potential effects on human health should be thoroughly researched before their use.
•
Using starter materials other than graphite will provide greener solutions for the preparation of GFNs.
•
All future toxicological studies must include a detailed physicochemical characterization of GFNs. They should be comprehensively described and characterized in terms of their size, morphological structure, surface area, charge, surface functionalization, purity, and aggregation/dispersion. These characteristics can have a significant impact on their biocompatibility and biosafety. For instance, the short- and long-term toxic effects of GO, as well as their oxidation degrees, should be considered for large-scale water disinfection. Long-term toxicity assessments instead of short-term studies are required. Furthermore, while functionalizing the surface of graphene-derived NMs can significantly improve their biocompatibility, the long-term stability of the surface coating must be assessed.
